summary: American architect known for his signature all-white building exteriors. Designed the Getty Center in Los Angeles and the Ara Pacis museum in Rome. Member of the New York Five (with Michael Graves and Peter Eisenman). Pritzker Prize winner. Associated with the 'Whites' faction in the Whites vs. Grays debate among postmodern architects.

works.name: Getty Center, Los Angeles
works.description: Meier's most frequently clued building. A multi-building campus in Los Angeles for the J. Paul Getty Trust, accessed via tram from the parking area. Features white travertine-clad exteriors with 16,000 tons of Italian limestone. Sits on a hill overlooking Los Angeles with a cactus garden. Houses Van Gogh's Irises and Alma-Tadema's Spring among other works. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Designed by Meier for the J. Paul Getty Trust in Los Angeles; accessed via tram
examples: The Getty Center was designed by this Pritzker-winning American architect
Richard Meier designed a multi-building campus to house the extensive art collection his namesake Trust purchased.

works.name: Museum of the Ara Pacis (Museo dell'Ara Pacis), Rome
works.description: Meier's museum built to house the Ara Pacis Augustae in Rome. Features a travertine-plated white exterior with a low-slope staircase and glass walls filled with argon to filter light. Replaced a 1937 design by Vittorio Ballio Morpurgo, whose wall inscribed with the Res Gestae is the only remaining element. Wikipedia

works.name: MACBA (Museu d'Art Contemporani de Barcelona)
works.description: Meier's museum of contemporary art in Barcelona, featuring a white porcelain-enameled steel exterior and glass-paved hallways. Called 'the pearl' due to its contrast with the nearby Gothic Quarter (Barri Gòtic). Wikipedia

works.name: Jubilee Church (Dio Padre Misericordioso), Rome
works.description: Meier's church in Rome located next to the Tor Tre Teste housing complex, consisting of three circular sectors with white concrete curved surfaces and glass. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: How Meier is identified and biographical context. His trademark is the use of white for building exteriors. Won the Pritzker Prize. Member of the New York Five (along with Michael Graves and Peter Eisenman), which was seen as the major embodiment of modernism. Represented the 'Whites' faction in the Whites vs. Grays architectural debate, opposing postmodern ornamentation. First moved to New York and worked for Marcel Breuer (Bauhaus). First all-black building is a glass residential tower at 685 First Avenue.

clues.clue: Trademark use of white for building exteriors
examples: This architect loves using the color white for the exteriors of his buildings
is seemingly obsessed with the color white
whose white colors are typical of his style

clues.clue: Won the Pritzker Prize
examples: The Getty Center was designed by this Pritzker-winning American architect

clues.clue: Member of the New York Five (with Michael Graves and Peter Eisenman); associated with pure modernism / 'Whites' faction
examples: Meier is a member of this group of architects, which came to be seen as the major embodiment of modernism after a contentious MOMA meeting and study. It also includes Michael Graves and Peter Eisenman.
One of these factions was exemplified by the pure modernism of Peter Eisenman and Richard Meier

clues.clue: Worked for Marcel Breuer (Bauhaus) when he first moved to New York
examples: When Richard Meier first moved to New York, he worked for Marcel Breuer, who created the famed Wassily Chair while on the faculty of this institution [Bauhaus] founded by Walter Gropius.

clues.clue: First all-black building: glass residential tower at 685 First Avenue
examples: His first all-black building, a glass residential tower, is under construction at 685 First Avenue.

comprehensive_summary:
Richard Meier is an American architect best known for his trademark use of white on building exteriors — a quality so consistent it is clued as his defining characteristic across nearly every question. He won the Pritzker Prize and is a member of the New York Five, a group of architects associated with pure modernism that also includes Michael Graves and Peter Eisenman. In the Whites vs. Grays architectural debate that followed the 1970s postmodern turn, Meier and the New York Five were identified as the 'Whites,' championing modernism against the postmodern ornamentation of the 'Grays' led by Robert Venturi. When Meier first arrived in New York, he worked for Marcel Breuer, who had been on the faculty of the Bauhaus (founded by Walter Gropius) and was known for the Wassily Chair.

Meier's most frequently clued building is the Getty Center in Los Angeles, a multi-building campus designed for the J. Paul Getty Trust. The complex is accessed via tram and features white travertine-clad exteriors; one question specifies that it used 16,000 tons of Italian limestone and has an elevated cactus garden overlooking Los Angeles. It houses notable works including Van Gogh's Irises and Alma-Tadema's Spring.

His second most prominent work is the Museum of the Ara Pacis in Rome, built to house the ancient altar commissioned by Augustus. The museum features a travertine-plated white exterior with a low-slope staircase and glass walls filled with argon to filter light. It replaced a 1937 structure by Vittorio Ballio Morpurgo, whose wall inscribed with the Res Gestae was the only surviving element.

In Barcelona, Meier designed the MACBA (Museu d'Art Contemporani de Barcelona), featuring a white porcelain-enameled steel exterior and glass-paved hallways. It is nicknamed 'the pearl' for its contrast with the nearby Gothic Quarter (Barri Gòtic). In Rome, Meier also designed the Jubilee Church (Dio Padre Misericordioso), located next to the Tor Tre Teste housing complex, featuring three circular sectors with white concrete curved surfaces and glass.

Other buildings frequently cited in clues include: the Atheneum in New Harmony, Indiana; the High Museum of Art in Atlanta; Frankfurt's Museum of Decorative Arts; the Sandra Day O'Connor Courthouse in Phoenix; the Rothschild Tower in Tel Aviv; a namesake Model Museum on Long Island; and the Westbeth Artist Community in Manhattan's West Village (converted from a former Bell Telephone labs building). One question also describes a house on a steep slope in Michigan with tubed white supports and large windows bursting from a dense treeline. His first all-black building — a departure from his white signature — is a glass residential tower under construction at 685 First Avenue in New York.
recursive_suggestions: New York Five — architectural group Meier belongs to, frequently clued; includes Michael Graves, Peter Eisenman, Charles Gwathmey, John Hejduk
Getty Center — highly clued as its own answerline (Los Angeles museums, J. Paul Getty)
Ara Pacis Augustae — the monument housed in Meier's Rome museum; frequently its own answerline
Marcel Breuer — Meier's early employer; Bauhaus connection
